cards.title: Hardware, Software and Data
lines: Hardware is the physical parts of a computer that you can touch.
Software is the set of programs that tell the hardware what to do.
System software runs the computer, such as the operating system.
Applications software helps the user carry out tasks, such as a word processor.
Analogue data is continuous, while digital data uses discrete values of 0 and 1.
A computer must convert analogue signals to digital before it can process them.

cards.title: CPU, RAM and ROM
lines: The CPU processes instructions and data to run programs.
RAM is volatile and temporarily stores data and programs currently in use.
ROM is non-volatile and stores the start-up instructions for the computer.
RAM can be read from and written to, but ROM is normally read only.
An operating system controls the hardware and provides an interface for the user.
A GUI uses windows, icons, and menus, while a CLI uses typed text commands.

cards.title: Input and Output Devices
lines: An input device sends data into a computer, such as a keyboard, mouse, or scanner.
An output device receives data from a computer, such as a monitor, printer, or speaker.
Direct data entry devices read data automatically, such as barcode readers.
A magnetic stripe reader reads data stored on the back of a card.
Direct data entry is fast and reduces errors from manual typing.
The wrong choice of device can slow down a task or cause more mistakes.

cards.title: Storage Devices and Media
lines: Magnetic storage such as a hard disk stores data on spinning magnetic platters.
Optical storage such as a CD or DVD is read and written using a laser.
Solid state storage such as an SSD or memory card uses flash memory with no moving parts.
Solid state storage is fast and durable but can cost more per gigabyte.
Optical media are cheap and portable but store less than a hard disk.
The best storage depends on the capacity, speed, portability, and cost required.

cards.title: Networks and Network Devices
lines: A LAN connects devices over a small area such as one building.
A WLAN is a wireless LAN that connects devices without cables.
A WAN connects devices over a large area, such as the internet.
A router directs data between networks and connects a network to the internet.
An intranet is a private network for use within one organisation.
An extranet lets trusted outside users access part of an organisation's network.

cards.title: Applications of ICT
lines: ATMs and internet banking let customers manage money without visiting a branch.
Expert systems store knowledge to help diagnose problems, for example in medicine.
POS terminals in shops read barcodes and update stock automatically.
Online booking systems let customers reserve travel and events over the internet.
Recognition systems include OMR, OCR, RFID, and biometric recognition.
GPS uses satellites to find the location of a device on Earth.

cards.title: Data Threats and Protection
lines: Hacking is gaining access to a computer system without permission.
Phishing uses fake emails or websites to steal personal details.
A virus is a program that can damage files or disrupt a computer.
Encryption scrambles data so it cannot be read without the correct key.
Two-factor authentication adds a second check beyond a password.
Biometrics use physical features such as fingerprints to verify identity.

cards.title: Health, Safety and eSafety
lines: Repetitive strain injury can be caused by long periods of typing or using a mouse.
Eye strain can be caused by staring at a screen for too long.
Taking regular breaks and using good posture helps prevent health problems.
eSafety means staying safe when using the internet, email, and social media.
You should not share personal information with people you do not know online.
Physical safety includes avoiding trailing cables and not overloading sockets.

cards.title: Databases and Web Development Layers
lines: A database stores data in tables made of records and fields.
A primary key is a field that uniquely identifies each record in a table.
A relationship links tables together using a primary key and a foreign key.
The content layer of a web page is built with HTML.
The presentation layer controls the look of a page and is built with CSS.
The behaviour layer controls how a page responds to the user.
